_comment: SFBB compiled content – Chilling and Cooking sections only. Inheritance rule: a safety point inherits the condition_id of its parent safe method unless it explicitly provides its own condition_id. Section condition_id applies to all safe methods under it, but each safe method can override.

safe_methods.safe_method_name: Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food
safe_methods._comment: Most safety points inherit stores_or_displays_chilled_food. Only those with a more specific condition (e.g., displays_chilled_food, handles_raw_fish) are listed with their own condition_id.

safety_points.text: Certain foods need to be kept chilled to keep them safe, for example: food with a 'use by' date, food that says 'keep refrigerated' on the label, food you have cooked and will not serve immediately, ready-to-eat food such as salads, cooked meats, sandwiches and cream-based desserts. If these types of food are not kept cold enough, harmful bacteria could grow.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Make sure you use food before its 'use by' date. Food with 'use by' dates, cooked dishes and other ready-to-eat food have a limited shelf life. Food cannot be supplied or served after its 'use by date'.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Follow the manufacturer's instructions on how to use fridges and chilled display equipment. It is important to use equipment properly to make sure food is kept cold enough. It is recommended that fridges and chilled display equipment should be set at 5°C or below. This is to make sure that chilled food is kept at 8°C or below.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food > Prove it

safety_points.text: It is important to keep chilled food cold while it is on display to prevent harmful bacteria from growing in the food. Pre-cool the display unit before you put chilled food in it. Only display as much food as you think you will need. Display food for the shortest time possible. You could also use a 'dummy' portion for display (which will not be eaten) or use photographs to show customers what the food looks like.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Some foods require storage at temperatures lower than 8°C to keep them safe, so always follow the manufacturer's storage guidance.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Minimise the time chilled foods are kept out at room temperature during preparation. You can help do this by only preparing small batches one at a time.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: "Food that needs extra care" guidance: If you buy fresh fish make sure you store it between 0°C and 4°C
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Harmful bacteria can grow in food that is left to chill slowly rather than chilled as quickly as possible. If you have cooked food that you will not serve immediately, chill it as quickly as possible, then put it in the fridge. Chilling food is a prerequisite for freezing. Freeze hot food as soon as it has been properly chilled.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Freezing > Safety Points

safety_points.text: Options for chilling down food you can use are: divide food into smaller portions, cut joints of meat in half, cover pans of hot food and move them to a colder area or stand them in cold water, stir food regularly while it is chilling down, spread food out on a tray, use a 'cool' setting on your oven, or consider using a blast chiller.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food

safety_points.text: Avoid cooking large quantities of food in advance, unless you need to. Large quantities of food are more difficult to chill down quickly, especially solid food.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food

safety_points.text: When you have just cooked the food, use a probe to test its temperature. Then test the temperature again at regular intervals to find out how fast the food is being chilled down. Remember to use a clean probe each time you check the food.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food > Check it

safety_points.text: "Food that needs extra care" guidance: When you have cooked rice, make sure you keep it hot until serving or chill it down as quickly as possible and then keep it in the fridge. You can chill down more quickly by dividing it into smaller portions, spreading it out on a clean tray, or running it under cold water (make sure the water is clean and drinking quality).
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food

safety_points.text:
Food should be thoroughly defrosted before cooking (unless the manufacturer's instructions tell you to cook from frozen or you have a proven safe method). If food is still frozen or partially frozen, it will take longer to cook. The outside of the food could be cooked, but the centre might not be, which means it could contain harmful bacteria.

The options to defrost food are:
- Ideally, plan ahead to leave enough time and space to defrost small amounts of food in the fridge. Putting food in the fridge will keep it at a safe temperature while it is defrosting.
- If you cannot defrost food in the fridge, you could put it in a container and then place it under cold running water. Cold water will help to speed up defrosting without allowing the outside of the food to get too warm. Raw meat and poultry (including large joints and whole birds) should not be defrosted under cold running water unless they are in a sealed container. For more information visit the FSA website.
- Defrost food in the microwave on the 'defrost' setting. This is a fast way to defrost food.
- If necessary, you could defrost food at room temperature. Follow the manufacturer's defrosting instructions. Food should be left out at room temperature for the shortest time possible. Ideally, defrost these foods in the fridge. Foods will defrost quite quickly at room temperature, but harmful bacteria could grow in food if it gets too warm while defrosting.
Keep meat/poultry separate from other food when it is defrosting, to prevent cross-contamination.
Once food has been defrosted, you should cook it until it is piping hot all the way through. After cooking, use the food immediately or chill or freeze it safely straight away.

Harmful bacteria could be spread, contaminating sinks, taps and surfaces. If you use the sink to defrost some food, make sure the sink is clean and empty. The sink should be cleaned and then disinfected after being used for defrosting. Cold running water will help speed up defrosting.

When you think food has defrosted, it is important to check to make sure. The outside may look defrosted, but the inside could still be frozen. Check for ice crystals in the food using your hand or a skewer. With birds, check the joints are flexible.
Food that must be kept frozen (e.g., ice cream) cannot be refrozen once it has started to defrost. You will have to use it immediately or throw it away.
Once the food is defrosted, its shelf life depends on the number of days left until expiry when it was frozen.

It is important to take care when freezing food and handle frozen food safely. Put frozen food in the freezer as soon as it is delivered. If frozen food starts to defrost, harmful bacteria could grow.

If you are freezing fresh food, freeze it as soon as it has been delivered or prepared. Freeze hot food as soon as it has been properly chilled. The longer you wait before freezing food, the greater the chance of harmful bacteria growing.

Divide food into smaller portions and put it in containers or freezer bags before freezing. Smaller portions will freeze (and defrost) more quickly. The centre of larger portions takes longer to freeze, allowing harmful bacteria to grow. Using containers and freezer bags prevents cross-contamination.
Food that must be kept frozen (e.g., ice cream) cannot be refrozen once it has started to defrost. You will have to use it immediately or throw it away.
'Food that needs extra care' guidance: Eggs, you can freeze them for later use if required.
'Food that needs extra care' guidance: If you buy frozen fish, then keep it frozen until you are ready to use it.
You can use the digital display, a dial thermometer or a probe thermometer to check that your freezer is keeping food at a safe temperature.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Freezing > Prove it

safety_points.text:
It is essential to cook food properly to kill any harmful bacteria. If it is not cooked properly, it might not be safe for your customers to eat.

Do not forget that cooking does not remove allergens from food, so you need to handle food that contains allergens carefully.

It is also very important to handle ready-to-eat food carefully to prevent contamination with harmful bacteria. This is because it will not be cooked or reheated before serving.
Where appropriate, follow the manufacturer's cooking instructions for food products. The manufacturer has tried and tested safe cooking methods specifically for its products.

Preheat equipment such as ovens and grills before cooking as per the manufacturer's instructions, if available. If you use equipment before it has preheated, food will take longer to cook. This means that recommended cooking times in recipes or manufacturers' instructions might not be long enough.

Do not let raw food touch or drip onto cooked food, e.g. when adding food to a grill/barbecue. Never use the same utensils, plates or containers for raw and cooked or ready-to-eat food.

Raw food can carry harmful bacteria, which could spread onto cooked food and make it unsafe.

It is a good idea to fully cook poultry in an oven first, then finish it on the barbecue. This will make sure that the poultry is cooked thoroughly. The meat should be cooked to the safest temperature, and not be pink or red. Juices should be clear, with no pink in red meat.

Check that whole birds are cooked to a safe temperature in the thickest area, which is between the leg and breast. The meat should not be pink or red, and the juices should be clear.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text: If you are using leftover marinade as a sauce, make sure it is cooked until steaming hot. Marinades can carry bacteria from the raw meat or poultry if not cooked thoroughly.

Make sure liquid dishes, e.g. gravy, soups, sauces and stews, are simmering and stir them frequently. Check that they bubble rapidly and reach a safe temperature in several places in case of cold spots before serving.

This is to make sure the food is hot enough to kill bacteria. Stirring will help ensure the food is the same temperature throughout.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
If you serve beef or lamb rare (whole cuts such as steaks and whole joints only), make sure all of the outside surfaces are fully cooked, e.g. by sealing in a pan.

This will kill harmful bacteria on the outside of the meat.

Whole cuts of meat, such as steaks and joints, only ever have bacteria on the outside surface of the meat.

Pork, any rolled joints of meat and processed meat products, such as sausages and burgers, should not be served rare. They should be cooked to a safe temperature in the centre with no pink or red in them or their juices.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
Liver and offal, including dishes such as liver pate or parfait, must be cooked to a safe temperature in the centre of the meat and until there is no pink meat left.

Harmful bacteria can be found in the centre of the liver as well as the outside.
Turn the meat during cooking. This helps it cook more evenly and thoroughly.

Use a disinfected temperature probe to check that dishes are properly cooked or reheated.

Examples of safe time/temperature combinations for cooking include:
- 80°C for at least 6 seconds
- 75°C for at least 30 seconds
- 70°C for at least 2 minutes
- 65°C for at least 10 minutes
- 60°C for at least 45 minutes
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Prove it

safety_points.text: The largest piece of meat in stews, curries, etc., should be cooked to a safe temperature in the centre with no pink or red.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text: Check that combination dishes (e.g., containing meat and vegetables) are cooked to a safe temperature in the centre. If you are cooking a large dish or batch, check in several places.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
Check the fish is cooked to a safe temperature in the centre and the colour and texture have changed.

Some fish (e.g. tuna) may be served 'rare' as long as they have been correctly frozen beforehand to kill any parasites which may be present and are fully seared on the outside to kill any harmful bacteria that may be present. Further guidance is available at the FSA website.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it
